responses.response: This fact is accepted as documented and it is precisely why S1's lottery-weight mechanism (year 2, step 2) targets admission gatekeeping directly rather than waiting for the S3 peer-effect question to resolve. Accepting that current selective-track admission proxies status rather than ability does not, on its own, license S3: it is an argument for fixing the SORTING MECHANISM now (S1's fixed lottery weight, hard SES-audit gate, maintainer-uniform rules), which addresses the exact harm A2 describes without betting the whole cohort's structure on an unverified symmetric/asymmetric peer-effects assumption. The governing-majority interest here is not indifference to A2's harm — it is refusal to buy an unverified structural fix (S3) when a verified, narrower fix (S1) targets the same documented mechanism at lower reversibility risk. If Hungarian evidence later shows that status-based sorting persists even after S1's coaching-dampening reform — i.e., that the admission-channel fix is necessary but not sufficient — that would be new information strengthening the case for S3, not an argument against pursuing S1 first.
responses.new_condition: Opposition to S3 would move toward conditional support if post-S1 evidence shows that fixing the admission-channel status-sorting (via lottery weight + SES audit) fails to close the equity gap, demonstrating that status-based sorting operates through channels beyond admission design — combined with the previously stated conditions (Hungarian peer-effect data, audited teacher-supply forecast, independent 7-year pilot).
